items.title: How SQL Server Stores Data: Pages, Extents, Heaps
items.bodyMarkdown:
# Storage Fundamentals

Everything in SQL Server lives on **8KB pages**. A row must fit on a page (~8060 bytes; larger values go off-row). Eight pages = one **extent** (64KB), the allocation unit.

Why you care: **I/O is counted in pages**. A query reading 10,000 rows scattered across 10,000 pages costs vastly more than the same rows packed into 100 pages. Every optimization ultimately reduces page reads.

## Heap vs clustered table

- **Heap** — table without a clustered index. Rows go wherever there's space; located via RID (file:page:slot).
- **Clustered table** — rows physically ordered by the clustering key in a B-tree.

Heaps look fine until: forwarding pointers from updates scatter reads; no efficient range scans; `TOP N ORDER BY` must sort everything.

## See it yourself (runnable on your LocalDB)

```sql
-- Pages consumed by each table in the current DB
SELECT t.name, p.rows, au.data_pages, au.data_pages * 8 / 1024.0 AS size_mb
FROM sys.tables t
JOIN sys.partitions p ON p.object_id = t.object_id AND p.index_id IN (0,1)
JOIN sys.allocation_units au ON au.container_id = p.partition_id
WHERE au.type = 1;

-- Logical reads per query: the number that matters most
SET STATISTICS IO ON;
SELECT * FROM LearningItems;
-- Messages tab: 'logical reads N' = pages touched
```

**Logical reads** (pages from memory) is your primary tuning metric — deterministic, unlike elapsed time which varies with load and caching.
items.practiceTask: Run both queries above against your LearnMore LocalDB database. Note logical reads for SELECT * FROM LearningItems vs SELECT Id, Title FROM LearningItems — then explain why they're the same (hint: row layout) but would differ with a covering index.

quiz.question: The most reliable metric for comparing two versions of a query is:
options: Logical reads — pages touched, independent of cache state and server load
quiz.explanation: Elapsed time varies with caching, load, and hardware; logical reads measures the work the plan actually does.

items.title: Clustered Indexes: The Table IS the Index
items.bodyMarkdown:
# Clustered Indexes

A clustered index **is the table** — leaf pages contain full rows, ordered by the key, organized as a B-tree. One per table (rows exist in one physical order).

```
        [root page]              ← level 2
       /          \
  [intermediate] [intermediate]  ← level 1: key ranges → child pages
   /    |    \    /    |    \
 [leaf][leaf][leaf][leaf][leaf]  ← level 0: the actual rows
```

Millions of rows → 3-4 levels. Finding a row by key = one page per level: 3-4 logical reads total. That's the O(log n) from your DSA lessons, with a huge branching factor.

## Choosing the clustering key — NUSE

| Property | Why |
|---|---|
| **N**arrow | The clustering key is stored in *every nonclustered index* — wide key bloats everything |
| **U**nique | Non-unique keys get a hidden 4-byte uniqueifier |
| **S**tatic | Updating the key physically moves the row and updates all NC indexes |
| **E**ver-increasing | New rows append at the end — no page splits mid-table |

`INT IDENTITY` / sequential IDs: textbook. **Random GUIDs (NEWID()): the classic disaster** — inserts land on random pages, splitting full pages constantly (fragmentation + write amplification). If you need GUIDs, use NEWSEQUENTIALID or a separate NC unique index over an int clustering key.

```sql
-- Watch the B-tree depth of your own tables
SELECT OBJECT_NAME(object_id) AS tbl, index_type_desc, index_depth, page_count
FROM sys.dm_db_index_physical_stats(DB_ID(), NULL, NULL, NULL, 'DETAILED')
WHERE index_level = 0 AND index_id = 1;
```

EF Core note: `Id` int primary key = clustered by convention. You're usually fine — just don't cluster on Guid Id without thinking.
items.practiceTask: Run the index_depth query on your LearnMore database. Then create a scratch table clustered on UNIQUEIDENTIFIER DEFAULT NEWID(), insert 100k rows, and check avg_fragmentation_in_percent vs an INT IDENTITY twin table.

quiz.question: Clustering on NEWID() GUIDs hurts because:
options: Random key values insert into random pages, causing constant page splits and fragmentation
quiz.explanation: Ever-increasing keys append neatly at the end; random keys force splits of already-full pages throughout the table.

items.title: Nonclustered Indexes and the Key Lookup Tax
items.bodyMarkdown:
# Nonclustered Indexes

A separate B-tree: leaf pages hold the **index key columns + the clustered key** (the 'row locator'), not the whole row. Up to 999 per table.

```sql
CREATE INDEX IX_Orders_CustomerId ON Orders (CustomerId);
-- leaf level effectively contains: (CustomerId, OrderId←clustering key)
```

## The Key Lookup tax

```sql
SELECT OrderDate, Total FROM Orders WHERE CustomerId = 42;
```

The index finds the matching OrderIds fast — but `OrderDate` and `Total` aren't in the index. For **each row**, SQL Server does a **Key Lookup** into the clustered index (3-4 reads each). 1,000 matching orders ≈ 4,000 extra reads.

Worse: if the optimizer estimates *many* lookups, it abandons the index entirely and **scans the table** — 'why isn't my index used?' — this is why.

## The fix: covering indexes with INCLUDE

```sql
CREATE INDEX IX_Orders_CustomerId_Covering
ON Orders (CustomerId)                 -- key: what you seek/sort on
INCLUDE (OrderDate, Total);            -- leaf-only payload: what you SELECT
```

Now the index answers the whole query — zero lookups. INCLUDE columns aren't sorted (cheap to maintain) and don't count against key size limits.

## Design rules

- Key columns: equality predicates first, then range/sort columns
- INCLUDE: the handful of columns the query selects
- Every index costs writes: INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE maintains all of them — indexes are bought with write speed and storage
- `SELECT *` makes covering impossible — another reason to project only needed columns (EF: `.Select(x => new {...})`)
items.practiceTask: In your LearnMore DB: SET STATISTICS IO ON, query DailyAssignments by Date selecting Status. Check reads. Create an index on (Date) INCLUDE (Status, LearningItemId), re-run, compare logical reads and the plan (lookup should vanish).

quiz.question: A query using a nonclustered index shows 'Key Lookup' per row in the plan. This means:
options: Columns needed by the query aren't in the index, forcing a clustered-index visit per row
quiz.explanation: The NC index only stores its keys + clustering key; missing columns require fetching the full row — fixable with INCLUDE.

quiz.question: INCLUDE columns differ from key columns in that they:
options: Exist only at the leaf level, unsorted — usable for covering but not seeking
quiz.explanation: INCLUDE adds payload for covering queries cheaply; only key columns drive seek/sort ability.

items.title: Reading Execution Plans: Your Debugger for SQL
items.bodyMarkdown:
# Execution Plans

The plan shows *how* SQL Server executes your query — the operator tree the optimizer chose. Reading plans is to SQL what reading stack traces is to C#.

## Getting plans

- SSMS: **Ctrl+M** (actual plan) then run; Ctrl+L for estimated
- `SET STATISTICS XML ON` for XML
- EF Core: log the SQL (`LogTo`/`ToQueryString`), paste into SSMS

**Prefer actual plans** — they include real row counts vs estimates, and that difference is diagnostic gold.

## How to read one

- Data flows **right to left**, top to bottom; each arrow = rows passing between operators
- **Arrow thickness** ≈ row count — a fat arrow into a Sort or Hash operator is a red flag
- Hover any operator: estimated vs **actual rows**. A 100× mismatch means stale statistics or an unSARGable predicate misleading the optimizer
- Operator **cost %** points at the expensive step (it's an estimate — trust actual rows more)

## The operators that matter first

| Operator | Meaning | Reaction |
|---|---|---|
| Clustered Index **Seek** | B-tree navigation to specific rows | 😊 usually optimal |
| Clustered Index **Scan** | reading the whole table | 😐 fine for small tables/reports; bad for selective lookups |
| Index Seek + **Key Lookup** | see previous lesson | consider INCLUDE |
| **Sort** | explicit sorting — memory or tempdb | can an index provide the order? |
| **Hash Match** | building hash table (join/aggregate) | fine, but memory-hungry at volume |

## Warnings (yellow ⚠ on operators)

Implicit conversions, tempdb spills, missing join predicates — always hover and read them; they're the plan telling you exactly what's wrong.
items.practiceTask: Capture the actual plan for your app's /api/stats queries (grab SQL from EF logging, run in SSMS with Ctrl+M). Identify: one seek or scan, the fattest arrow, and any estimate-vs-actual mismatch. Write one sentence on each.

quiz.question: In an actual execution plan, estimated rows = 1 but actual rows = 50,000 on an operator. This usually indicates:
options: Stale statistics or a predicate the optimizer can't estimate — the plan shape was chosen for the wrong data volume
quiz.explanation: The optimizer picked operators (e.g., nested loops) believing 1 row would flow; 50,000 rows through that shape is a performance disaster.

items.title: Seeks vs Scans: Selectivity Decides
items.bodyMarkdown:
# Seek vs Scan

**Seek**: navigate the B-tree to a key range — cost proportional to rows *returned*.
**Scan**: read every leaf page — cost proportional to table *size*.

## Neither is universally good

| Query returns | Better plan |
|---|---|
| 5 rows of 10M | Seek, obviously |
| 9M rows of 10M | **Scan** — seeking 9M rows one at a time would be far slower |

The optimizer weighs **selectivity**: roughly, if a predicate matches more than a few percent of a large table, scanning sequential pages beats millions of random B-tree navigations. The tipping point (~1-3% with lookups involved) explains many 'SQL ignored my index' mysteries.

## Why your index gets ignored — checklist

1. **Low selectivity** — `WHERE IsActive = 1` matching 95% of rows: scan wins, correctly
2. **Key Lookup cost** — index isn't covering and too many rows match (previous lesson)
3. **UnSARGable predicate** — function wrapped around the column (dedicated lesson coming)
4. **Stale statistics** — optimizer misjudges selectivity
5. **Tiny table** — a 2-page table is cheaper to scan than seek; ignore index advice on small tables

```sql
-- Force-compare (diagnostics ONLY — hints don't belong in production code)
SELECT * FROM Orders WHERE Status = 'Open';                          -- optimizer's choice
SELECT * FROM Orders WITH (FORCESEEK) WHERE Status = 'Open';        -- forced seek
-- Compare logical reads with SET STATISTICS IO ON: the optimizer is usually right
```

**Residual predicate** trap: a seek on `CustomerId` with a hidden filter on `Status` inside means the index served only half the WHERE — check the seek operator's 'Predicate' (vs 'Seek Predicate') in the plan.
items.practiceTask: Run the FORCESEEK comparison on a low-selectivity predicate against a scratch table with 100k rows (e.g., Status with 3 values). Record logical reads both ways and confirm the optimizer chose correctly.

quiz.question: WHERE IsDeleted = 0 matches 97% of a 10M-row table. The optimizer scans despite an index on IsDeleted. Why is it right?
options: Retrieving 9.7M rows via seek + lookups costs far more random I/O than one sequential scan
quiz.explanation: Seeks win when returning few rows; for most-of-the-table results, sequential scanning is the cheaper access path.

items.title: SARGability: Don't Wrap Columns in Functions
items.bodyMarkdown:
# SARGable Predicates

**S**earch **ARG**umentable = the predicate can use an index seek. The rule: **the column stands alone on one side; computation goes to the other side**.

## The killers (all force scans)

```sql
-- ❌ function on the column: must evaluate for EVERY row
WHERE YEAR(OrderDate) = 2026
WHERE UPPER(Email) = 'X@Y.COM'
WHERE LTRIM(RTRIM(Code)) = 'AB12'
WHERE CAST(CreatedAt AS date) = '2026-07-05'
WHERE Total * 1.14 > 1000
WHERE ISNULL(Status, 'None') = 'Open'
WHERE LastName LIKE '%son'          -- leading wildcard: no B-tree entry point

-- ✅ SARGable rewrites: same logic, seekable
WHERE OrderDate >= '2026-01-01' AND OrderDate < '2027-01-01'
WHERE Email = 'x@y.com'                          -- rely on CI collation
WHERE CreatedAt >= '2026-07-05' AND CreatedAt < '2026-07-06'
WHERE Total > 1000 / 1.14
WHERE (Status = 'Open')                          -- handle NULL logic explicitly
WHERE LastName LIKE 'John%'                      -- trailing wildcard seeks fine
```

The date range rewrite (`>= start AND < next-day`) is the single most common fix — memorize the open-upper-bound form; `BETWEEN` with dates invites 23:59:59.997 edge bugs.

## Why it matters doubly

UnSARGable predicates don't just force scans — they **wreck cardinality estimates** (optimizer can't use statistics through a function), causing bad joins downstream.

## EF Core angle

```csharp
// ❌ translates to unSARGable SQL
.Where(o => o.CreatedAt.Year == 2026)
// ✅
.Where(o => o.CreatedAt >= start && o.CreatedAt < end)
```

Escape hatch when you truly need computation: a **persisted computed column** with an index on it, or (SQL 2022+) indexed exact-match via trailing computed columns.
items.practiceTask: Create a 500k-row scratch table with a DATETIME2 column + index. Compare logical reads and plans: WHERE YEAR(col)=2026 vs the range rewrite. Then grep your work codebase for '.Year ==' in EF queries.

quiz.question: WHERE CAST(CreatedAt AS date) = '2026-07-05' scans despite an index on CreatedAt because:
options: The function must run on every row's value before comparing — the B-tree order of raw values can't be used
quiz.explanation: Index entries are ordered by CreatedAt, not by CAST(CreatedAt AS date); rewrite as a half-open range to seek.

quiz.question: Which LIKE pattern can use an index seek?
quiz.explanation: A known prefix maps to a B-tree range; leading wildcards give no entry point (consider full-text search for those).

items.title: Implicit Conversions: The Silent Scan Generator
items.bodyMarkdown:
# Implicit Conversions

When predicate types mismatch, SQL Server converts one side automatically — governed by **data type precedence**. If the *column* gets converted, every row is converted before comparison: **scan**, exactly like wrapping it in a function yourself.

## The classic .NET-developer case

```sql
-- Column is VARCHAR(20); parameter arrives as NVARCHAR (default for .NET strings!)
SELECT * FROM Customers WHERE AccountNumber = @p1  -- @p1 NVARCHAR
-- nvarchar > varchar in precedence → the COLUMN is converted:
-- CONVERT_IMPLICIT(nvarchar, AccountNumber) = @p1 → index useless
```

ADO.NET/Dapper send C# strings as NVARCHAR by default. Against VARCHAR columns = silent table scans. **EF Core handles this** when the model knows the column type (`HasColumnType("varchar(20)")` / scaffolded correctly) — raw ADO.NET/Dapper code must specify `DbType.AnsiString`.

## Spotting it

- Plan operator warning: ⚠ `CONVERT_IMPLICIT(...)` "may affect cardinality estimate / seek plan"
- Plan XML: search `PlanAffectingConvert`

```sql
-- Find plans in cache suffering from it
SELECT TOP 20 qs.execution_count, qt.text
FROM sys.dm_exec_query_stats qs
CROSS APPLY sys.dm_exec_sql_text(qs.sql_handle) qt
CROSS APPLY sys.dm_exec_query_plan(qs.plan_handle) qp
WHERE CAST(qp.query_plan AS NVARCHAR(MAX)) LIKE '%PlanAffectingConvert%';
```

## Fix directions

1. **Match types at the source** — parameter type = column type (best)
2. Standardize schema on NVARCHAR if you genuinely need Unicode (accepting 2× storage)
3. Explicitly convert the *parameter*, never the column: `WHERE AccountNumber = CAST(@p1 AS VARCHAR(20))`

Same trap variants: `INT` parameter vs `VARCHAR` column (`WHERE Code = 123` — column converted to int, plus conversion-failure risk), and joining tables whose FK/PK types drifted apart.
items.practiceTask: Create a table with a VARCHAR(20) indexed column. From C# (raw SqlCommand), query it with AddWithValue (sends NVARCHAR) vs SqlDbType.VarChar. Capture both plans and find the CONVERT_IMPLICIT warning.

quiz.question: A .NET string parameter queries a VARCHAR column and causes a scan because:
options: .NET sends NVARCHAR; precedence forces the VARCHAR column to convert per-row, killing the seek
quiz.explanation: Higher-precedence nvarchar wins the conversion; converting the column side is unSARGable. Set DbType.AnsiString or fix the model.

items.title: Statistics: What the Optimizer Actually Knows
items.bodyMarkdown:
# Statistics

The optimizer never looks at your data while planning — it consults **statistics**: histograms (up to 201 steps) describing value distribution per column/index, plus density info for multi-column correlation.

Every cost decision — seek vs scan, join type, memory grants, operator order — flows from **cardinality estimates** derived from these histograms. Garbage stats → garbage plans.

```sql
-- Inspect a statistic's histogram
DBCC SHOW_STATISTICS ('LearningItems', 'IX_LearningItems_TopicId_Title');
-- Or the modern way:
SELECT * FROM sys.dm_db_stats_histogram(OBJECT_ID('LearningItems'), 2);

-- When were stats last updated, and how much changed since?
SELECT s.name, sp.last_updated, sp.rows, sp.rows_sampled, sp.modification_counter
FROM sys.stats s
CROSS APPLY sys.dm_db_stats_properties(s.object_id, s.stats_id) sp
WHERE OBJECT_NAME(s.object_id) = 'DailyAssignments';
```

## Maintenance reality

- Auto-create (on predicate columns) and auto-update are **on by default** — leave them on
- Auto-update triggers after ~`SQRT(1000 × rows)` modifications (modern default) — for big tables that's still a lot of drift
- Nightly `UPDATE STATISTICS` / Ola Hallengren maintenance is standard practice for busy databases
- `UPDATE STATISTICS t WITH FULLSCAN` when sampled stats mislead on skewed data

## Symptoms of stale/misleading stats

- Actual rows ≫ estimated rows in plans (the mismatch you learned to spot)
- A query suddenly slow after a big data load (stats describe yesterday's data)
- **Ascending date problem**: new rows beyond the histogram's last step → estimates of ~1 row for 'today's data' queries

Skewed data (one customer owns 60% of orders) defeats averages — that's the parameter sniffing lesson next.
items.practiceTask: Run DBCC SHOW_STATISTICS on an index in your LearnMore DB. Read the histogram: find RANGE_HI_KEY, EQ_ROWS. Insert 1000 rows into DailyAssignments (script dates), check modification_counter, run a query, and see if auto-update fired (last_updated changes).

quiz.question: The optimizer decides seek vs scan and join types based on:
options: Live sampling of table data during compilation
quiz.explanation: Planning consults statistics, never the actual data — which is why stale or skew-blind stats produce bad plans.

items.title: Parameter Sniffing: One Plan, Many Parameter Values
items.bodyMarkdown:
# Parameter Sniffing

On first execution of a parameterized query/proc, SQL Server **sniffs the actual parameter value**, builds the optimal plan *for that value*, and **caches it for everyone**.

Usually good (plans tailored to real values). With **skewed data**, it's a time bomb:

```sql
-- Customer 1: 3 orders. Customer 2: 4,000,000 orders.
CREATE PROC GetOrders @CustomerId INT AS
SELECT * FROM Orders WHERE CustomerId = @CustomerId;

EXEC GetOrders 1;   -- plan: index seek + key lookups (perfect for 3 rows)
EXEC GetOrders 2;   -- reuses that plan: 4M key lookups ≈ 16M reads 💀
-- If proc compiled with @CustomerId=2 first: scan plan — wasteful for customer 1 but survivable.
```

Signature symptom: **"the same query is fast sometimes, slow sometimes"** or "it got slow suddenly, and a restart/rebuild fixed it" (plan got evicted and recompiled with a different sniffed value).

## Fix toolbox (know the trade-offs)

| Fix | Effect | Cost |
|---|---|---|
| `OPTION (RECOMPILE)` | fresh optimal plan per execution | compile CPU each run — fine for infrequent heavy queries |
| `OPTIMIZE FOR (@p = value)` / `UNKNOWN` | plan for a chosen/average value | someone's still suboptimal |
| Recode: IF/ELSE into two procs | separate plans per branch | code duplication |
| Covering index removing lookups | both plans converge to seek | best when feasible |
| SQL 2022+ **PSP optimization** | multiple plans per query, per cardinality bucket | needs dbcompat 160 |

```sql
-- Diagnose: compare sniffed vs actual
-- In the actual plan XML: ParameterCompiledValue vs ParameterRuntimeValue
```

**Not** a fix: `DBCC FREEPROCCACHE` in production — it's a defibrillator, not a treatment.

EF Core relevance: LINQ queries are parameterized (good — plan reuse), which means they're sniffable. The same skewed-tenant problem hits multi-tenant apps hard: tenant with 100 rows vs tenant with 10M sharing plans.
items.practiceTask: Build the skew scenario on LocalDB: Orders table, customer A with 10 rows, customer B with 500k. Parameterized query via sp_executesql; execute A-first then B, check reads; clear the plan (sp_recompile), execute B-first then A. Record the four timings/read counts.

quiz.question: A proc is fast for months, then suddenly slow for everyone until someone reruns it after a restart. Likeliest cause:
options: Plan eviction + recompilation with an atypical sniffed parameter value on a skewed column
quiz.explanation: The cached plan lottery: whoever triggers compilation donates their parameter's plan to everyone — with skew, that plan can be terrible for the majority.

items.title: Join Operators: Nested Loops, Hash, Merge
items.bodyMarkdown:
# Physical Join Operators

`JOIN` in SQL is logical; the optimizer picks one of three physical algorithms — the same algorithms from your DSA lessons:

## Nested Loops

For each outer row, seek/scan the inner table. Your DSA nested-for-loop, but the inner side can *seek*.

- **Great**: small outer input + indexed inner side (OLTP point queries: order + its 5 lines)
- **Terrible**: large outer input (10k outer rows × inner seek = 10k seeks) — this is what a bad cardinality estimate produces

## Hash Match

Build a hash table on the smaller input, probe with the larger — your Dictionary-based join.

- **Great**: large unsorted, unindexed inputs (reporting, ETL)
- **Costs**: memory grant; spills to tempdb when the estimate was too low (⚠ warning in plan)
- Blocking-ish: build phase must finish before output starts

## Merge Join

Two sorted inputs walked with two pointers — your merge-sort merge step.

- **Great**: both inputs already sorted by join key (clustered indexes on both PK/FK)
- If a Sort operator appears just to feed a merge join, the optimizer decided sorting was still cheaper — but question it

## Reading trouble

| Symptom in plan | Meaning |
|---|---|
| Nested Loops with fat outer arrow | estimate was wrong (expected few, got many) — stats/SARGability issue |
| Hash Match spill warning | memory grant too small — cardinality underestimate |
| Unexpected Sort before Merge | maybe missing index that would provide order |

```sql
-- Watch the optimizer switch strategies as volume grows
SELECT o.*, c.Name FROM Orders o JOIN Customers c ON c.Id = o.CustomerId WHERE o.Id = 5;          -- loops
SELECT o.*, c.Name FROM Orders o JOIN Customers c ON c.Id = o.CustomerId;                          -- hash/merge
```

You don't choose join types (hints exist; avoid) — you **enable good choices** with accurate statistics, SARGable predicates, and useful indexes.
items.practiceTask: On your scratch Orders/Customers tables, capture plans for: single-order join (expect loops), full-table join (expect hash or merge). Then drop the FK index and watch the plan change. Note which DSA algorithm each operator corresponds to.

quiz.question: Nested Loops joining 50,000 outer rows against an inner seek performs poorly because:
options: 50,000 separate inner-side B-tree navigations (random I/O) — hash would build once and probe
quiz.explanation: Loops shine with small outer inputs; at volume, per-row seeks lose to one hash build + streaming probe.

items.title: Key Lookups at Scale & the Tipping Point
items.bodyMarkdown:
# The Tipping Point

You know Key Lookups (lesson 3). Here's the systemic view: for a non-covering index, the optimizer estimates lookup cost as **random I/O per row**. At some estimated row count — often just **1-3% of the table** — the math flips and a full scan becomes cheaper. That's the tipping point.

## Consequences you'll observe

1. **Same query, different plans by parameter**: `WHERE Status='Rare'` seeks; `WHERE Status='Common'` scans. Correct behavior, confusing without this mental model.
2. **Index 'randomly' unused**: the estimate crossed the threshold — maybe because stats drifted.
3. **Parameter sniffing amplifier**: sniffed rare value → seek+lookup plan → reused for common value → catastrophic lookups (the previous lesson's bomb, mechanism now clear).

## Quantify a lookup's cost

```sql
SET STATISTICS IO ON;
-- Non-covering: seek + N lookups
SELECT Id, CustomerName, Total FROM Orders WHERE Region = 'West';
-- reads ≈ (seek pages) + N × (CI depth ~3)
```

1,000 rows via lookups ≈ 3,000+ reads; the same rows from a covering index ≈ 10-20 reads; scanning 50k pages might beat 20k lookups — now the optimizer's choice makes sense.

## Decision framework

| Situation | Action |
|---|---|
| Hot query, few SELECT columns | Add INCLUDE — kill the lookups |
| Wide SELECT (many columns) | Fix the query first: project less (`SELECT *` can't be covered) |
| Reporting query hitting 30% of table | Accept the scan; consider columnstore (later lesson) |
| Lookup on rarely-run query | Leave it — indexes cost writes; not every query deserves one |

Every index is a bet: read savings must outweigh permanent write tax + storage. The tipping point tells you when the bet pays.
items.practiceTask: On a 500k-row scratch table with a non-covering index on Region: find the tipping point experimentally — query increasingly common Region values until the plan flips from seek+lookup to scan. Record the row percentage where it flipped.

quiz.question: The 'tipping point' where SQL Server abandons a seek+lookup plan for a scan is typically around:
options: A few percent of the table — random lookups are that expensive
It never abandons an available index
quiz.explanation: Each lookup costs ~CI-depth random reads; sequential scanning wins surprisingly early — often at 1-3% selectivity.

items.title: Paging Done Right: OFFSET, Keyset, and COUNT
items.bodyMarkdown:
# Pagination Performance

## OFFSET/FETCH — fine until page 1000

```sql
SELECT Id, Title, CreatedAt FROM Articles
ORDER BY CreatedAt DESC, Id DESC
OFFSET 990 ROWS FETCH NEXT 10 ROWS ONLY;
```

OFFSET must **read and discard** all skipped rows: page 1 reads 10 rows, page 100 reads 1,000, page 10,000 reads 100,010. Linear degradation — the deep-page timeout classic. (EF's `.Skip().Take()` generates exactly this.)

## Keyset (seek) pagination — constant time per page

Remember the last row seen; seek directly past it:

```sql
SELECT TOP 10 Id, Title, CreatedAt FROM Articles
WHERE (CreatedAt, Id) < (@lastCreatedAt, @lastId)   -- conceptually; T-SQL spells it:
-- WHERE CreatedAt < @lastCreatedAt OR (CreatedAt = @lastCreatedAt AND Id < @lastId)
ORDER BY CreatedAt DESC, Id DESC;
```

With an index on `(CreatedAt DESC, Id DESC)`: every page = one seek + 10 rows, page 1 or page 100,000 identical cost.

Trade-offs: no 'jump to page 57' (only next/prev), needs a **deterministic unique sort** (the Id tiebreaker is mandatory — without it rows can repeat/vanish across pages). Perfect for infinite scroll and APIs (`?after=cursor`).

## The hidden second query: COUNT(*)

Total-count-for-pager often costs more than the page itself (full index scan). Options: cache the count; return 'has more' instead of total (fetch pageSize+1 rows); estimate from `sys.dm_db_partition_stats` for admin dashboards.

## EF Core

```csharp
// keyset
.Where(a => a.CreatedAt < lastCreated || (a.CreatedAt == lastCreated && a.Id < lastId))
.OrderByDescending(a => a.CreatedAt).ThenByDescending(a => a.Id).Take(10)
```

Always `OrderBy` before `Skip/Take` — EF throws otherwise, because SQL without ORDER BY has **no defined row order**.
items.practiceTask: Create 1M-row scratch table. Compare logical reads: OFFSET page 1 vs page 50,000 vs keyset equivalent of page 50,000. Then implement keyset paging for one list endpoint in any project.

quiz.question: OFFSET 100000 ROWS FETCH NEXT 10 gets slower as the offset grows because:
options: All 100,000 skipped rows are still read and discarded server-side
quiz.explanation: OFFSET is 'read and throw away', not 'jump to' — keyset pagination seeks directly instead.

quiz.question: Keyset pagination requires the ORDER BY to include a unique tiebreaker column because:
options: Ties in the sort column make 'after this row' ambiguous — rows can repeat or vanish between pages
quiz.explanation: Deterministic total order is what makes the 'seek past last seen row' predicate correct.

items.title: Isolation Levels: What Locks Are Held When
items.bodyMarkdown:
# Transaction Isolation Levels

Isolation levels trade **consistency anomalies** against **concurrency**. Know the anomaly each level eliminates:

| Level | Dirty read | Non-repeatable read | Phantom | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| READ UNCOMMITTED | possible | possible | possible | no shared locks — reads in-flight data |
| READ COMMITTED (default) | ✗ | possible | possible | shared lock per row, released immediately |
| REPEATABLE READ | ✗ | ✗ | possible | shared locks held to txn end |
| SERIALIZABLE | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | + range locks on scanned ranges |
| SNAPSHOT | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | row versions in tempdb, no read locks; write-conflict errors |

## The anomalies in business terms

- **Dirty read**: report includes an order whose transaction rolls back — money that never existed
- **Non-repeatable read**: same row read twice in one txn differs (balance check → debit uses changed value)
- **Phantom**: re-running a range query finds new rows (audit count ≠ detail rows)

## What NOLOCK actually means

`WITH (NOLOCK)` = READ UNCOMMITTED per table. Beyond dirty reads: page splits during your scan can make you **skip rows or read rows twice**. Acceptable for approximate monitoring dashboards; indefensible for anything financial or user-facing correctness. It's tech debt with a cute name.

```sql
-- See current sessions' isolation levels
SELECT session_id, transaction_isolation_level  -- 1=RU, 2=RC, 3=RR, 4=Serializable, 5=Snapshot
FROM sys.dm_exec_sessions WHERE session_id = @@SPID;
```

EF Core: `BeginTransaction(IsolationLevel.Snapshot)` sets per transaction. Next lesson: RCSI — the setting that changes the default game.
items.practiceTask: Two SSMS windows on LocalDB: window A starts a transaction updating a row without committing; window B reads it under READ COMMITTED (blocks) then WITH (NOLOCK) (returns uncommitted data). Roll back A and confirm B's NOLOCK read reported data that never existed.

quiz.question: Beyond dirty reads, NOLOCK scans can also:
options: Skip rows or count rows twice when page splits relocate data mid-scan
quiz.explanation: Allocation-order scans without locks race against page splits — rows move under the scan and get missed or double-read.

items.title: RCSI and Snapshot: Readers Stop Blocking Writers
items.bodyMarkdown:
# Row Versioning: RCSI & Snapshot Isolation

Classic READ COMMITTED: readers take shared locks → **readers block writers, writers block readers**. The endless blocking-chain misery of busy OLTP.

**READ COMMITTED SNAPSHOT (RCSI)** changes the mechanism, not the level: readers get the **last committed version** of each row from the tempdb version store — no shared locks at all.

```sql
ALTER DATABASE MyDb SET READ_COMMITTED_SNAPSHOT ON WITH ROLLBACK IMMEDIATE;
-- Check:
SELECT name, is_read_committed_snapshot_on, snapshot_isolation_state_desc FROM sys.databases;
```

| | Classic RC | RCSI |
|---|---|---|
| Reader hits row being updated | **blocks** until commit | reads prior committed version instantly |
| Writer hits row being read | blocks | proceeds |
| Writer vs writer | blocks | still blocks (unchanged) |

Azure SQL Database has RCSI **on by default**; on-prem defaults off — one of the biggest 'why is prod different' surprises.

## Costs & gotchas

- **tempdb pressure**: version store lives there; long-running transactions pin versions (monitor `sys.dm_tran_version_store_space_usage`)
- 14 bytes/row versioning overhead
- **Semantic change**: queries read a *point-in-time* view. Rare patterns relying on blocking-as-coordination (queue tables doing `UPDATE ... WHERE Status='New'` races) need `UPDLOCK`/`READPAST` hints instead

## Full SNAPSHOT isolation (the stricter sibling)

Entire *transaction* sees one consistent point-in-time; concurrent committed writes to data you then update → **error 3960 update conflict** (retry needed). Use for long consistent reads (reports spanning many tables); RCSI for general OLTP.

For your ASP.NET Core apps: RCSI + short transactions eliminates the majority of read/write blocking with almost no code change — usually the single highest-leverage concurrency fix.
items.practiceTask: Enable RCSI on a scratch LocalDB database. Repeat the previous lesson's two-window experiment: reader no longer blocks on the in-flight update and sees the old committed value. Then check version store usage DMV during the open transaction.

quiz.question: Under RCSI, a SELECT hitting a row mid-update by another transaction:
options: Returns the last committed version from the version store immediately
quiz.explanation: Row versioning serves readers a consistent committed snapshot — no shared locks, no blocking.

items.title: Blocking and Locking: Diagnosis First Aid
items.bodyMarkdown:
# Locking & Blocking

Locks are how SQL Server serializes access: **S** (shared, reads), **X** (exclusive, writes), **U** (update — S that intends to become X, deadlock-avoidance), plus intent locks (IS/IX) marking 'something below is locked' at page/table level.

**Blocking** = waiting for an incompatible lock. Normal in small doses; pathological when transactions are long or touch more than needed.

## First-aid diagnosis kit

```sql
-- Who blocks whom, with statements
SELECT r.session_id, r.blocking_session_id, r.wait_type, r.wait_time,
       t.text, r.status
FROM sys.dm_exec_requests r
CROSS APPLY sys.dm_exec_sql_text(r.sql_handle) t
WHERE r.blocking_session_id <> 0;

-- The head of the blocking chain (block others, not blocked themselves)
SELECT s.session_id, s.login_name, s.host_name, s.program_name,
       (SELECT text FROM sys.dm_exec_sql_text(c.most_recent_sql_handle)) AS last_sql
FROM sys.dm_exec_sessions s
JOIN sys.dm_exec_connections c ON c.session_id = s.session_id
WHERE s.session_id IN (SELECT blocking_session_id FROM sys.dm_exec_requests WHERE blocking_session_id <> 0)
  AND s.session_id NOT IN (SELECT session_id FROM sys.dm_exec_requests WHERE blocking_session_id <> 0);
```

The classic head-blocker: an **idle session with an open transaction** (`sleeping`, `open_transaction_count > 0`) — an app that began a transaction and went off to call an API or wait for a user.

## Lock escalation

~5,000 locks on one object → SQL Server swaps them for one **table lock**. Your 100k-row batch delete just serialized the whole table. Fix: chunk large modifications (WHILE loop, 1-4k rows per transaction).

## Prevention beats diagnosis

1. **Short transactions** — never hold one across network calls (the #1 rule). EF: `SaveChanges` late, once
2. Touch rows in **consistent order** across code paths
3. Index your **foreign keys** — deleting a parent scans the child table *inside the transaction* without one
4. RCSI (previous lesson) for the read/write half of the problem
items.practiceTask: Create a blocking chain on LocalDB (window A: BEGIN TRAN + UPDATE, no commit; windows B, C: touch the same row). Run the diagnosis queries, identify the head blocker, then demonstrate lock escalation by updating 10,000 rows in one transaction and checking sys.dm_tran_locks for an object-level X lock.

quiz.question: The most common head of a long blocking chain in web apps is:
options: An idle session holding an open transaction while the app does non-database work
quiz.explanation: Locks live until COMMIT/ROLLBACK; a transaction held across an API call or user think-time blocks everyone behind it.

quiz.question: Un-indexed foreign keys cause blocking on parent DELETEs because:
options: Verifying no children reference the parent requires scanning the entire child table inside the transaction
quiz.explanation: The FK check needs child rows by parent ID; without an index that's a full scan holding locks — index every FK column.

items.title: Deadlocks: Reading the Graph, Breaking the Cycle
items.bodyMarkdown:
# Deadlocks

Session A holds lock 1, wants lock 2; session B holds lock 2, wants lock 1. Neither can proceed — SQL Server detects the cycle (checks every ~5s) and **kills the cheaper victim** with error **1205**.

Deadlock ≠ blocking: blocking resolves when the holder commits; deadlock never would — hence the kill.

## Get the deadlock graph (no setup needed)

```sql
-- system_health Extended Events session records them automatically
SELECT CAST(event_data AS XML).query('(event/data/value/deadlock)[1]') AS graph
FROM (
  SELECT CAST(target_data AS XML) AS td
  FROM sys.dm_xe_session_targets st
  JOIN sys.dm_xe_sessions s ON s.address = st.event_session_address
  WHERE s.name = 'system_health' AND st.target_name = 'ring_buffer') x
CROSS APPLY td.nodes('//RingBufferTarget/event[@name="xml_deadlock_report"]') AS t(event_data);
```

Read the XML/graph: two **process** nodes (the queries), two **resource** nodes (the locks), arrows showing who owns and who waits. The victim has the ✖.

## Classic causes & cures

| Cause | Cure |
|---|---|
| Two code paths lock the same rows in **opposite order** (A: Orders→Customers; B: Customers→Orders) | Lock in one canonical order everywhere |
| Read-then-update races (`SELECT` then `UPDATE` same row from two sessions) | `UPDLOCK` hint on the initial read: `SELECT ... WITH (UPDLOCK)` takes U lock upfront |
| Key lookup deadlocks (seek on NC index + update of CI in crossfire) | Covering index removes the lookup leg |
| Long transactions widening the window | Shorten; batch |

## Application side (EF Core / ADO.NET)

Error 1205 is **retryable by design** — the victim should retry the whole transaction:

```csharp
// EnableRetryOnFailure retries connection faults but NOT deadlocks by default;
// wrap deadlock-prone units in an explicit retry (Polly) catching SqlException 1205.
```

A deadlock a week: normal noise. A deadlock storm on one code path: read the graph — it names the exact statements; fix ordering or add the UPDLOCK.
items.practiceTask: Manufacture a deadlock on LocalDB: two windows, each BEGIN TRAN, update row 1 in A and row 2 in B, then cross (A updates row 2, B updates row 1). Retrieve the graph from system_health and identify both resources and the victim.

quiz.question: The canonical deadlock prevention for code paths touching the same pair of tables is:
options: Acquire locks in the same consistent order in every code path
quiz.explanation: Cycles require opposite acquisition orders; one canonical order makes cycles impossible (retry remains the safety net).

items.title: tempdb, Sorts, and Spills: The Hidden Workhorse
items.bodyMarkdown:
# tempdb and Memory Grants

tempdb is one shared database doing everyone's dirty work: sort/hash overflow, version store (RCSI!), temp tables, table variables, triggers' inserted/deleted, online index builds.

## Memory grants and spills

Before executing, each query gets a **memory grant** sized from cardinality estimates for its Sorts and Hash operations. Estimate too low → the operation **spills**: partitions get written to tempdb and processed in passes. Plans show ⚠ 'Operator used tempdb to spill data'.

Spill chain of causation you now fully understand:
**bad stats / unSARGable predicate → wrong row estimate → small grant → tempdb spill → slow query + tempdb I/O storm**

```sql
-- Spills in recent plans (Query Store, SQL 2017+)
SELECT TOP 20 qt.query_sql_text, rs.avg_tempdb_space_used
FROM sys.query_store_runtime_stats rs
JOIN sys.query_store_plan p ON p.plan_id = rs.plan_id
JOIN sys.query_store_query q ON q.query_id = p.query_id
JOIN sys.query_store_query_text qt ON qt.query_text_id = q.query_text_id
WHERE rs.avg_tempdb_space_used > 0 ORDER BY rs.avg_tempdb_space_used DESC;
```

## Fixing sorts (often better than fixing spills)

The cheapest Sort is no Sort: an index on the ORDER BY columns delivers rows pre-sorted. Check plans for Sort operators feeding TOP — `ORDER BY CreatedAt DESC` + matching index = Sort disappears entirely.

## Temp tables vs table variables (interview staple)

| | #temp table | @table variable |
|---|---|---|
| Statistics | ✅ yes | ❌ none (est. 1 row pre-2019; deferred compilation helps after) |
| Indexes | any | inline PK/unique only |
| Recompiles | can cause | fewer |
| Verdict | >100 rows or joined | tiny row counts only |

The '@table variable joined to a million-row table' disaster: optimizer plans for 1 row, picks nested loops, gets 100k — now you know all three lessons behind why.
items.practiceTask: Force a spill on LocalDB: sort a 1M-row scratch table on an unindexed wide column and find the spill warning in the actual plan. Add the index, re-run, confirm the Sort operator vanished. Then join a 100k-row @table variable vs #temp table to a table and compare plans.

quiz.question: A Hash Match spills to tempdb primarily because:
options: The memory grant — sized from cardinality estimates — was too small for actual row volume
The table has no clustered index
quiz.explanation: Underestimates produce undersized grants; the overflow partitions to tempdb — fix the estimate, fix the spill.

items.title: Query Store: Your Performance Flight Recorder
items.bodyMarkdown:
# Query Store

Built-in per-database recorder of **queries, plans, and runtime stats over time**. The answer to "it was fast yesterday — what changed?" (On by default SQL 2022+ / Azure SQL; enable everywhere else.)

```sql
ALTER DATABASE MyDb SET QUERY_STORE = ON;
ALTER DATABASE MyDb SET QUERY_STORE (OPERATION_MODE = READ_WRITE,
    QUERY_CAPTURE_MODE = AUTO, MAX_STORAGE_SIZE_MB = 1000);
```

## What it answers

| Question | SSMS report / query |
|---|---|
| What got slower recently? | **Regressed Queries** |
| What's most expensive overall? | Top Resource Consumers (by CPU/reads/duration) |
| Did the plan change? When? | Tracked Queries — plan timeline |
| Different plans for same query? | plan_id history per query_id |

## The killer feature: plan forcing

Parameter-sniffing regression at 2 AM? Find the query, see the good old plan and the bad new plan side by side, click **Force Plan** — the optimizer must use the chosen plan until you unforce. A surgical rollback, buying time to fix root cause properly.

```sql
EXEC sp_query_store_force_plan @query_id = 42, @plan_id = 118;
-- Later: sp_query_store_unforce_plan
```

## Practical notes

- Runtime stats aggregate per interval (default 60 min) — fine for trends
- `QUERY_CAPTURE_MODE = AUTO` skips trivial queries (right default)
- It's also the data source for **automatic plan correction** (`FORCE_LAST_GOOD_PLAN`) on Enterprise/Azure
- EF Core apps benefit hugely: parameterized LINQ queries aggregate cleanly by query_id, so your worst endpoints surface fast

Interview one-liner: "First thing I check for a sudden regression: Query Store plan history — did the plan flip, and does forcing the previous plan restore performance?"
items.practiceTask: Enable Query Store on your LearnMore LocalDB. Run the app, browse around, then open Top Resource Consumers in SSMS and identify your app's heaviest query. Check whether it has multiple plans.

quiz.question: A query regressed after a plan change last night. Query Store's fastest mitigation is:
options: Force the previously good plan for that query while you fix the root cause
quiz.explanation: Plan forcing pins the known-good plan surgically — no restart, no cache-wide side effects, reversible.

items.title: Wait Statistics: What Is SQL Server Waiting For?
items.bodyMarkdown:
# Wait Statistics

Every moment a request isn't executing on CPU, it's **waiting** on something — and SQL Server records what. Wait stats are the top-down entry point for 'the server is slow' (vs plan analysis for 'this query is slow').

```sql
-- Aggregate waits since restart (filter the benign noise)
SELECT TOP 15 wait_type, wait_time_ms / 1000.0 AS wait_sec,
       signal_wait_time_ms * 100.0 / NULLIF(wait_time_ms,0) AS signal_pct
FROM sys.dm_os_wait_stats
WHERE wait_type NOT IN ('SLEEP_TASK','BROKER_TO_FLUSH','LAZYWRITER_SLEEP','XE_TIMER_EVENT',
    'REQUEST_FOR_DEADLOCK_SEARCH','CHECKPOINT_QUEUE','WAITFOR','SP_SERVER_DIAGNOSTICS_SLEEP',
    'HADR_FILESTREAM_IOMGR_IOCOMPLETION','QDS_PERSIST_TASK_MAIN_LOOP_SLEEP','DIRTY_PAGE_POLL')
ORDER BY wait_time_ms DESC;
```

## The translation table

| Wait type | Means | First reaction |
|---|---|---|
| `PAGEIOLATCH_SH/EX` | waiting for pages from **disk** | queries reading too much (missing indexes) or too little RAM |
| `LCK_M_*` | lock waits — **blocking** | blocking-chain lesson toolkit; RCSI; shorter txns |
| `CXPACKET`/`CXCONSUMER` | parallelism coordination | normal in moderation; with skew, check estimates/MAXDOP |
| `PAGELATCH_*` (not IO!) | **memory** page contention — often tempdb allocation | classic tempdb contention; more tempdb files (pre-2019) |
| `WRITELOG` | transaction log flush | log disk latency; too many tiny transactions — batch |
| `SOS_SCHEDULER_YIELD` + high `signal_pct` | CPU pressure | plan cache CPU hogs; reduce compilation |
| `ASYNC_NETWORK_IO` | client slow to consume rows | app streaming huge results — page/limit; not a SQL problem! |

`ASYNC_NETWORK_IO` deserves a highlight for app developers: it usually means **your app requested a huge result set** and processes it row by row. Fix the query/paging, not the server.

## Method

1. Snapshot waits → identify dominant category
2. Correlate with Query Store top consumers in that dimension (reads for PAGEIOLATCH, duration for locks)
3. Fix the queries; re-baseline (`DBCC SQLPERF('sys.dm_os_wait_stats', CLEAR)` on dev)

Waits tell you *which lesson of this course to apply*.
items.practiceTask: Run the wait stats query on your LocalDB after exercising the app. Then generate ASYNC_NETWORK_IO: SELECT a large table from C# reading rows with Thread.Sleep(1) per row, and watch the wait counter climb — proof it's a client-side pattern.

quiz.question: Dominant ASYNC_NETWORK_IO waits usually indicate:
options: The client app fetches large result sets and consumes them slowly — fix the app's query size/processing
quiz.explanation: SQL is waiting for the client to take rows off the wire — almost always oversized results or row-by-row client processing.

items.title: Missing Index DMVs: Free Advice, Read Critically
items.bodyMarkdown:
# Missing Index Suggestions

SQL Server logs every time a query *wished* an index existed:

```sql
SELECT TOP 20
    CONVERT(DECIMAL(12,1), migs.avg_total_user_cost * migs.avg_user_impact * (migs.user_seeks + migs.user_scans)) AS score,
    mid.statement AS table_name,
    mid.equality_columns, mid.inequality_columns, mid.included_columns,
    migs.user_seeks, migs.last_user_seek
FROM sys.dm_db_missing_index_groups mig
JOIN sys.dm_db_missing_index_group_stats migs ON migs.group_handle = mig.index_group_handle
JOIN sys.dm_db_missing_index_details mid ON mid.index_handle = mig.index_handle
ORDER BY score DESC;
```

Column meanings → index shape: **equality_columns** first (key), then **inequality_columns** (key), **included_columns** → INCLUDE.

## Why you must NOT create them blindly

- Suggestions are **per-query myopic**: 20 near-duplicate suggestions for one table (only column order/includes differ) should become **one consolidated index**
- No awareness of **existing indexes** — often a suggestion = existing index + 1 include column → *modify*, don't add
- No write-cost consideration: 15 indexes on a hot OLTP table tax every INSERT/UPDATE
- Column order within equality_columns is **not optimized** — put the most selective/most-filtered-alone column first yourself

## The audit habit (both directions)

```sql
-- Unused/write-only indexes: candidates for removal (stats since restart!)
SELECT OBJECT_NAME(s.object_id) AS tbl, i.name,
       s.user_seeks + s.user_scans + s.user_lookups AS reads, s.user_updates AS writes
FROM sys.dm_db_index_usage_stats s
JOIN sys.indexes i ON i.object_id = s.object_id AND i.index_id = s.index_id
WHERE s.database_id = DB_ID() AND i.type_desc = 'NONCLUSTERED'
ORDER BY reads ASC, writes DESC;
```

High writes + zero reads (over a *representative* period — beware restarts and rare-but-critical month-end reports) = the index costs and never pays.

Healthy ritual: monthly — consolidate missing-index suggestions, drop confirmed dead weight, re-check Query Store impact.
items.practiceTask: Run both DMV queries against a database your team uses (or your LearnMore DB after heavy app use). Consolidate any duplicate suggestions into one index definition on paper, and identify one unused-index removal candidate with justification.

quiz.question: Creating every missing-index suggestion verbatim is wrong because:
options: Suggestions are per-query and overlap heavily; they ignore existing indexes and write costs — consolidate instead
quiz.explanation: The DMV is a wishlist, not a design: merge near-duplicates, extend existing indexes, and weigh the write tax.

items.title: Columnstore Indexes: Analytics at 10-100x
items.bodyMarkdown:
# Columnstore Indexes

Rowstore pages store complete rows — perfect for OLTP point queries. Analytics queries ("SUM revenue by month over 100M rows, 3 columns of 40") pay to read every column anyway. **Columnstore** stores each column separately in compressed ~1M-row segments.

## Why it's 10-100× for aggregation

1. **Column elimination**: read only the 3 referenced columns — 90%+ I/O gone
2. **Extreme compression** (10× typical): repetitive column data compresses beautifully; more data per read
3. **Batch mode execution**: operators process ~900-row vectors instead of row-at-a-time
4. **Segment elimination**: per-segment min/max metadata skips whole segments (`WHERE Year=2026` skips 2024 segments)

```sql
-- Analytics copy of a big fact table
CREATE NONCLUSTERED COLUMNSTORE INDEX NCCI_Orders_Analytics
ON Orders (OrderDate, CustomerId, Region, Total, Quantity);
-- OLTP keeps its rowstore B-trees; reporting queries use the NCCI automatically
```

| Type | What | Use |
|---|---|---|
| Clustered CS | the table itself, columnar | pure fact/history tables, DW |
| **Nonclustered CS** | additional columnar copy | **HTAP**: OLTP table + real-time analytics — the sweet spot for app databases |

## Limits and care

- Point lookups/seeks: bad — that's rowstore's job (hence hybrid)
- Trickle updates create **delta rowgroups** (uncompressed B-tree overflow) — degrades until tuple mover compresses; bulk loads of ≥102,400 rows go straight to compressed segments
- Monitor: `sys.dm_db_column_store_row_group_physical_stats` for open/deleted rowgroup bloat; REORGANIZE to force compression

Dashboard queries aggregating millions of rows while OLTP hums along on the same table — this is the feature. Your reporting endpoints (like this app's /api/stats at scale) are exactly the shape it serves.
items.practiceTask: Load 2M rows into a scratch Sales table on LocalDB. Compare logical reads + elapsed for SUM(Total) GROUP BY Month: plain rowstore vs after adding a nonclustered columnstore. Check the plan for 'Batch' execution mode.

quiz.question: A nonclustered columnstore index on an OLTP table enables:
options: Real-time analytics on live data — columnar scans for aggregates while B-trees serve OLTP
quiz.explanation: HTAP: one table, two storage formats — the optimizer routes point queries to rowstore and big aggregations to columnstore.

items.title: EF Core Performance I: Killing N+1 and Over-Fetching
items.bodyMarkdown:
# EF Core Query Hygiene

The ORM writes your SQL — these habits decide whether it writes good SQL.

## N+1: the classic

```csharp
var orders = await db.Orders.Take(100).ToListAsync();          // 1 query
foreach (var o in orders)
    Console.Write(o.Customer.Name);   // lazy loading: +100 queries 💀
```

Fixes, in order of preference:

```csharp
// 1. Projection — fetch exactly what the screen needs (no entities, no tracking)
var rows = await db.Orders.Take(100)
    .Select(o => new OrderRowDto(o.Id, o.Customer.Name, o.Total))
    .ToListAsync();                       // ONE query with a JOIN

// 2. Eager load when you need real entities
var orders = await db.Orders.Include(o => o.Customer).Take(100).ToListAsync();
```

## Over-fetching: SELECT * disease

Full entities drag every column (including that NVARCHAR(MAX) Description) plus change-tracking overhead. **Projection (`Select`) is the single highest-impact EF habit** — less I/O, covering-index-friendly SQL, no tracking.

## AsNoTracking for reads

```csharp
db.Orders.AsNoTracking()...   // no snapshot per entity; big win on large reads
// or projections — DTOs are never tracked anyway
```

## Cartesian explosion with multiple Includes

```csharp
db.Blogs.Include(b => b.Posts).Include(b => b.Tags)   // rows = posts × tags per blog!
    .AsSplitQuery()                                    // → separate queries per collection
```

Watch for it when including **two+ collections**; `AsSplitQuery` trades one bloated result set for multiple clean ones.

## See what EF sends (non-negotiable habit)

```csharp
query.ToQueryString();                        // exact SQL, paste into SSMS + Ctrl+M
builder.LogTo(Console.WriteLine, LogLevel.Information);   // or watch live
```

Every lesson in this track applies to EF output: SARGable lambdas, covering indexes for hot projections, keyset paging — the ORM is just a SQL generator you steer.
items.practiceTask: Turn on LogTo in a project. Find one endpoint doing N+1 or full-entity fetching for a list screen; rewrite with projection; compare query count and payload before/after (and ToQueryString both versions in SSMS).

quiz.question: The highest-impact default habit for EF read endpoints is:
options: Projecting to DTOs with Select — fetching only needed columns, untracked, in one query
quiz.explanation: Projection eliminates over-fetching, tracking overhead, and most N+1s in one move, and produces index-friendly SQL.

quiz.question: Include(b => b.Posts).Include(b => b.Tags) can explode row counts because:
options: One JOINed result multiplies collections: rows = |Posts| × |Tags| per blog — use AsSplitQuery
quiz.explanation: Two sibling collection JOINs produce a cartesian product per parent; split queries avoid the multiplication.

items.title: EF Core Performance II: SaveChanges, Bulk Ops, Compiled Queries
items.practiceTask: Benchmark three ways to deactivate 50k rows on LocalDB: tracked foreach+SaveChanges, ExecuteUpdateAsync, and raw SQL. Record elapsed time and SQL round-trips (LogTo) for each.

quiz.question: ExecuteUpdateAsync beats load-modify-SaveChanges for mass updates because:
options: It issues one set-based UPDATE without materializing or tracking entities
quiz.explanation: Set-based SQL does the work where the data lives; the tracked approach pays materialization, memory, and per-entity statement costs.

items.title: Index Maintenance and Fill Factor: What Still Matters
items.bodyMarkdown:
# Fragmentation, Rebuilds, and Modern Reality

## Two kinds of fragmentation

- **Logical**: page order on disk ≠ index order (page splits insert out-of-sequence pages). Hurts large sequential scans on **spinning disks**; nearly irrelevant on SSDs for typical OLTP
- **Low page density**: pages half-empty after splits/deletes — this one still matters everywhere: same rows, more pages, more reads, more buffer pool waste

```sql
SELECT OBJECT_NAME(ips.object_id) AS tbl, i.name,
       ips.avg_fragmentation_in_percent, ips.avg_page_space_used_in_percent, ips.page_count
FROM sys.dm_db_index_physical_stats(DB_ID(), NULL, NULL, NULL, 'SAMPLED') ips
JOIN sys.indexes i ON i.object_id = ips.object_id AND i.index_id = ips.index_id
WHERE ips.page_count > 100
ORDER BY ips.avg_fragmentation_in_percent DESC;
```

## The modern stance (post-SSD)

Obsessive nightly rebuilds are legacy cargo cult. What matters:

1. **Page density** (`avg_page_space_used`) — low density = real wasted I/O
2. **Statistics freshness** — half the historical 'rebuild fixed it!' wins were actually the free stats update (FULLSCAN) a rebuild includes
3. REBUILD vs REORGANIZE: rebuild = new index (offline unless Enterprise ONLINE=ON, updates stats); reorganize = in-place shuffle (always online, no stats update, interruptible)

Sensible defaults: Ola Hallengren's IndexOptimize weekly; thresholds ~30% frag AND >1000 pages; prioritize UPDATE STATISTICS daily on hot tables over rebuilding.

## Fill factor: pre-paying for inserts

`FILLFACTOR = 90` leaves 10% free per leaf page at rebuild time — room for future rows, fewer page splits on **random-insert** patterns (that GUID key you were warned about, or indexed columns that update frequently).

Trade: 10% more pages to read *all the time*. Default 100 (0) is right for ever-increasing keys; lower it only for indexes with **measured** split pain (`sys.dm_db_index_operational_stats` leaf_allocation_count).
items.practiceTask: Run the fragmentation query on your LearnMore DB. Then create the GUID-clustered scratch table again, insert 200k rows, record density/fragmentation, rebuild WITH (FILLFACTOR = 90), insert 50k more, and compare split behavior (leaf_allocation_count) vs FILLFACTOR 100.

quiz.question: On SSD-backed OLTP, the fragmentation metric that still genuinely wastes resources is:
options: Low avg_page_space_used_in_percent — half-empty pages inflate reads and buffer pool usage
quiz.explanation: Random I/O penalty of logical fragmentation faded with SSDs; paying for empty page space did not.

items.title: Transaction Log and Batching Large Modifications
items.bodyMarkdown:
# The Transaction Log

Every modification writes to the log **first** (write-ahead logging) — commit isn't done until the log record hits disk. The log is sequential-write and per-database single stream: it's often the true ceiling on write throughput (`WRITELOG` waits).

## Recovery models

| Model | Log behavior | Point-in-time restore | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| SIMPLE | truncates at checkpoint | ❌ | dev, ETL staging, rebuild-able data |
| FULL | kept until **log backup** | ✅ | production OLTP |

The classic incident: FULL recovery + no log backups → log grows until disk full. Log backups (every 5-15 min in prod) are what truncate it. `DBCC SQLPERF(LOGSPACE)` / `sys.dm_db_log_space_usage` to check.

## Why one giant DELETE is a disaster

`DELETE FROM History WHERE CreatedAt < @cutoff` on 50M rows:
- All 50M row-deletes logged in **one transaction** — massive log growth (can't truncate past an active transaction)
- Lock escalation → table lock → app blocked for the duration
- Rollback (if killed) takes as long as the delete did — or longer

## The batching pattern (memorize)

```sql
DECLARE @batch INT = 4000;
WHILE 1 = 1
BEGIN
    DELETE TOP (@batch) FROM History WHERE CreatedAt < @cutoff;
    IF @@ROWCOUNT < @batch BREAK;
    -- each iteration = its own transaction: log truncates (with backups),
    -- locks stay row/page level, other work interleaves
END
```

4000 stays under the ~5000 lock-escalation threshold. Add `WAITFOR DELAY '00:00:01'` between batches on busy systems. Same pattern for UPDATEs (`UPDATE TOP...` with a filter that excludes already-updated rows) — and for EF: loop `ExecuteDeleteAsync` on `.Take(4000)` (EF8+) or raw SQL.

Archival alternative when deleting *most* of a table: copy keepers to a new table, swap names — logging proportional to survivors instead of victims. Partition switching (Enterprise) makes it metadata-instant.
items.practiceTask: On LocalDB (SIMPLE recovery): insert 1M rows, then compare deleting 500k in one statement vs 4000-row batches — watch sys.dm_db_log_space_usage during each and check sys.dm_tran_locks for escalation in the single-statement version.

quiz.question: Batched deletes (TOP 4000 in a loop) beat one giant DELETE because:
options: Each small transaction allows log truncation, avoids lock escalation, and lets other work interleave
quiz.explanation: Small transactions bound log growth, stay under the escalation threshold, and make rollback/interruption cheap.

items.title: Computed Columns and Indexed Views
items.bodyMarkdown:
# Precomputing: Columns and Views

When a query keeps computing the same expression, store the computation.

## Computed columns

```sql
ALTER TABLE Orders ADD TotalWithTax AS (Total * 1.14) PERSISTED;
CREATE INDEX IX_Orders_TotalWithTax ON Orders (TotalWithTax);
```

- Non-persisted: computed on read (no storage)
- **PERSISTED**: stored, maintained on write — and **indexable** (deterministic expressions only)

The SARGability escape hatch: `WHERE Total * 1.14 > 1000` is unSARGable, but with the indexed computed column the optimizer **matches the expression automatically** and seeks — you don't even have to reference the new column.

Other uses: extracting a JSON property (`JSON_VALUE(Doc,'$.customerId') AS CustomerId PERSISTED`) then indexing it; normalized search columns (`LOWER(Email)`).

## Indexed views: materialized aggregates

```sql
CREATE VIEW dbo.vSalesByDay WITH SCHEMABINDING AS
SELECT OrderDate, COUNT_BIG(*) AS Cnt, SUM(ISNULL(Total,0)) AS Revenue
FROM dbo.Orders GROUP BY OrderDate;

CREATE UNIQUE CLUSTERED INDEX IX_vSalesByDay ON dbo.vSalesByDay (OrderDate);
```

The aggregate is now **stored and maintained** by SQL Server on every Orders write. A dashboard summing millions of rows becomes a seek into pre-aggregated data — and (Enterprise/modern editions) the optimizer can auto-match base-table queries to it.

Costs and constraints: every write to Orders also updates the view (write tax on hot tables); SCHEMABINDING locks schema; long requirements list (COUNT_BIG required with GROUP BY, no OUTER JOIN, deterministic everything, two-part names...).

## Choosing

| Need | Tool |
|---|---|
| Expression per row, filtered/sorted often | Persisted computed column + index |
| Pre-aggregated totals maintained real-time | Indexed view |
| Analytics at scale, tolerate batch-mode scans | Columnstore (often simpler than indexed views) |
| Can tolerate staleness | Scheduled summary table refresh — cheapest writes |
items.practiceTask: Add a persisted computed column extracting a value you filter on (e.g., LOWER(Email) or a JSON property) to a scratch table, index it, and confirm expression auto-matching: the original unSARGable WHERE now produces a seek.

quiz.question: An indexed view differs from a regular view in that it:
options: Runs its SELECT faster at query time
Physically stores and continuously maintains its result set, like a table
quiz.explanation: Regular views are inlined query text; an indexed view is materialized data updated on every base-table write — read speed bought with write cost.

items.title: Table Partitioning: Managing Big Tables by Slices
items.bodyMarkdown:
# Table Partitioning

Split one huge table into slices by a key (almost always **date**), transparent to queries. Available in all editions since SQL 2016 SP1.

```sql
CREATE PARTITION FUNCTION pfMonthly (datetime2)
AS RANGE RIGHT FOR VALUES ('2026-01-01','2026-02-01','2026-03-01', ...);

CREATE PARTITION SCHEME psMonthly AS PARTITION pfMonthly ALL TO ([PRIMARY]);

CREATE TABLE Events (...) ON psMonthly(CreatedAt);
```

## What it's actually for: **data lifecycle management**

- **Sliding window archival**: `SWITCH` a whole month out to an archive table = **metadata operation, instant** — vs the batched-DELETE lesson's minutes/hours. This is THE killer feature
- Loading: stage a month, SWITCH it in — bulk load without touching the live table
- Partition-level maintenance: rebuild/compress only recent partitions; older ones on cheaper filegroups

## What it's NOT (the #1 misconception)

**Partitioning is not primarily a query-speed feature.** Partition elimination (scanning only relevant partitions when the WHERE filters on the partitioning key) helps scans — but a good **index** usually helps more. A seek on a non-partitioned 500M-row table is still 4-5 reads. Queries that *don't* filter on the partition key can even get slightly slower (per-partition B-trees to probe).

## Gotchas

- Unique indexes must include the partitioning key (or be non-aligned, losing SWITCH)
- `$PARTITION.pfMonthly(CreatedAt)` to see row placement per partition
- Always keep empty partitions at both ends; automate `SPLIT`/`MERGE` maintenance (forgetting = giant catch-all last partition)

```sql
-- Row counts per partition
SELECT p.partition_number, p.rows
FROM sys.partitions p WHERE p.object_id = OBJECT_ID('Events') AND p.index_id IN (0,1);
```

Decision rule: reach for partitioning when **retention/archival/load management** on 50M+ row tables hurts — not because queries are slow.
items.practiceTask: On LocalDB: create a monthly-partitioned Events table with 4 partitions, load rows across months, verify placement with $PARTITION, then SWITCH the oldest partition to an archive table and time it against deleting the same rows.

quiz.question: The primary production justification for table partitioning is:
options: Instant metadata-level archival/load via partition SWITCH, and per-slice maintenance
quiz.explanation: It's a data-management feature; query gains are situational while SWITCH-based lifecycle operations are transformative.

items.title: Memory & the Buffer Pool: Why Cold Queries Are Slow
items.bodyMarkdown:
# The Buffer Pool

SQL Server reads pages through RAM: every page touched is cached in the **buffer pool** (the majority of SQL Server's memory). Logical read = page from RAM; **physical read** = disk → RAM first (10-1000× slower).

```sql
SET STATISTICS IO ON;
-- run twice: first run shows physical reads, second only logical — the cache warmed
```

This explains everyday mysteries:
- "Query is slow first time each morning" — cold cache (plus maybe overnight maintenance evicted pages)
- "Dev is faster than prod" — dev's tiny data fits entirely in RAM
- "Adding RAM fixed everything" — the working set finally fit

## Page Life Expectancy & memory pressure

```sql
SELECT object_name, counter_name, cntr_value
FROM sys.dm_os_performance_counters
WHERE counter_name IN ('Page life expectancy','Buffer cache hit ratio');

-- What's occupying the buffer pool, per table
SELECT OBJECT_NAME(p.object_id) AS tbl, COUNT(*) * 8 / 1024 AS mb_cached
FROM sys.dm_os_buffer_descriptors bd
JOIN sys.allocation_units au ON au.allocation_unit_id = bd.allocation_unit_id
JOIN sys.partitions p ON p.partition_id = au.container_id
WHERE bd.database_id = DB_ID()
GROUP BY p.object_id ORDER BY mb_cached DESC;
```

PLE crashing after a specific query = that query flushed everyone's hot pages by scanning something huge — **one bad scan taxes every other query on the server**. (Now the full damage model of missing indexes is visible: not just that query's reads, but cache eviction for all.)

## App-side implications

- Reading only needed columns/rows (projections! covering indexes!) = fewer pages cached = more of *everything else* stays hot
- That 'harmless' hourly `SELECT *` report over 20GB is a server-wide cache bomb — columnstore or replica it
- `MAX_SERVER_MEMORY`: always cap it (leave OS 4GB+); default unlimited fights the OS
items.practiceTask: On LocalDB: restart the instance (cold cache), run a table-scanning query with SET STATISTICS IO ON and note physical reads; re-run for logical-only. Then run the buffer-descriptor query to see your LearnMore tables' cache residency.

quiz.question: A giant table scan degrades OTHER queries' performance because:
options: It holds locks on their tables
It evicts their hot pages from the shared buffer pool — they now pay physical reads
quiz.explanation: The buffer pool is shared; one scan's flood of pages pushes out everyone's working set — cache pollution.

items.title: TVPs and Set-Based Thinking: Stop Calling SQL in Loops
items.practiceTask: Find a loop-with-query in any codebase you own (foreach + await db.*). Rewrite set-based (Contains, TVP, or ExecuteUpdate). Measure round-trips before/after with LogTo or SQL Profiler-style logging.

quiz.question: TVPs beat giant IN(...) lists for thousands of values because:
options: One cached parameterized plan, real join semantics, no SQL-text/parameter-count limits
quiz.explanation: Each distinct IN-list length is a distinct plan polluting the cache; a TVP is one stable, joinable table parameter.

items.title: A Query Tuning Methodology: The Checklist
items.bodyMarkdown:
# The Tuning Checklist

Everything in this track, ordered into the sequence to apply when something is slow.

## 0. Reproduce & measure honestly

```sql
SET STATISTICS IO, TIME ON;   -- logical reads = your currency
-- Actual execution plan on (Ctrl+M)
```

Get the *real* SQL (EF: `ToQueryString`). Establish the baseline number before touching anything.

## 1. Is the query asking for too much?

- `SELECT *`? Columns nobody reads? → project
- Rows the app filters afterward? → filter in SQL
- Huge result the user never scrolls? → page (keyset)
- Same data every request? → cache it; the fastest query is none

## 2. Can the engine find the rows efficiently?

- Plan: Scan where you expected Seek?
- Predicates SARGable? (functions on columns, implicit conversion warnings)
- Index exists for the WHERE + covers the SELECT? (missing index DMV as *input*, then design)
- Estimates ≈ actuals? If wildly off → statistics, or predicate opacity

## 3. Are the operators sane?

- Nested Loops with a fat outer arrow → estimate problem upstream
- Sort that an index could provide → index the ORDER BY
- Hash spill warnings → estimates again (it's always estimates)
- Key Lookup × thousands → INCLUDE columns

## 4. Is it even the query?

- Blocked, not slow: `sys.dm_exec_requests` wait type (LCK_*) → blocking toolkit
- Server-wide pressure: wait stats triage → PAGEIOLATCH/WRITELOG/CPU lessons
- Regression: Query Store plan history → sniffing? force last good plan as tourniquet

## 5. Only then: structural weapons

Covering/filtered indexes → computed columns → indexed views → columnstore → partitioning (lifecycle, remember, not speed) → read replicas / caching tiers.

## The meta-rules

1. **One change at a time**, re-measure logical reads after each
2. The fix is usually in steps 1-2 (the query), not step 5 (the infrastructure)
3. Keep a before/after log — numbers convince teammates, feelings don't
4. If reads dropped 100× but it's still 'slow', the bottleneck moved: network, app serialization, rendering — go profile there
items.practiceTask: Take the slowest endpoint you own. Run the full checklist top to bottom, documenting each step's finding and the logical-reads number after each change. Produce the before/after table.

quiz.question: According to the methodology, before creating any index for a slow query you should first:
options: Verify the query requests only needed rows/columns and its predicates are SARGable
quiz.explanation: Indexing a wasteful query cements the waste; shrink and SARG-ify the ask first — often no new index is needed.

items.title: Filtered Indexes and Index Strategy Review
items.bodyMarkdown:
# Filtered Indexes

An index over a **subset of rows** — a WHERE clause in the index definition:

```sql
-- 95% of orders are 'Closed'; queries always want the open 5%
CREATE INDEX IX_Orders_Open ON Orders (CustomerId) INCLUDE (Total)
WHERE Status = 'Open';
```

Benefits: fraction of the size (faster seeks, tiny maintenance cost — closed orders never touch it), stats over just the interesting rows (better estimates on skew!), and cheap uniqueness tricks:

```sql
-- 'unique when not null' — the classic
CREATE UNIQUE INDEX UX_Users_NationalId ON Users (NationalId) WHERE NationalId IS NOT NULL;

-- 'only one active default per customer'
CREATE UNIQUE INDEX UX_Addr_Default ON Addresses (CustomerId) WHERE IsDefault = 1;
```

That second one enforces a **business rule the schema otherwise can't express** — a constraint disguised as an index.

## Gotchas

- The query's predicate must **provably imply** the filter (`WHERE Status = 'Open'` matches; `WHERE Status = @s` does NOT — parameter could be anything → filtered index unusable with parameterized status!) — the big one for EF apps
- Workaround when values are known: `OPTION (RECOMPILE)` lets the optimizer see the runtime value
- SET options requirements (ANSI_NULLS etc.) — modern drivers comply by default

## Full index strategy in five lines

1. Cluster on narrow/static/increasing (usually the int PK) — every NC index carries it
2. Index every **foreign key** (join + delete-check performance)
3. For each *hot* query pattern: key = equality cols then range/sort col, INCLUDE = selected cols
4. Prefer **consolidation**: one index serving five queries beats five near-duplicates (leftmost-prefix rule: (A,B,C) also serves (A) and (A,B))
5. Filtered for hot subsets and conditional uniqueness; review usage DMVs quarterly; every index is a write tax — justify or drop
items.practiceTask: Add a filtered unique index expressing a real business rule in your schema ('one default X per Y', 'unique when not null'). Then demonstrate the parameterization gotcha: show the filtered index used with a literal but ignored with a parameter, and fix with OPTION(RECOMPILE).

quiz.question: A filtered index WHERE Status='Open' is ignored by WHERE Status=@status because:
options: The optimizer must build a plan valid for ANY @status value — it can't assume 'Open'
quiz.explanation: Cached plans must be correct for every parameter value; only literals (or RECOMPILE-time known values) provably satisfy the filter.

items.title: Concurrency Control in Apps: Optimistic vs Pessimistic
items.bodyMarkdown:
# Application Concurrency Patterns

Two users load the same record; both edit; both save. Who wins? Without a strategy: **last writer silently destroys the first's changes** — the lost update.

## Optimistic concurrency (default choice for web apps)

Assume conflicts are rare; **detect** rather than prevent:

```csharp
public class Order
{
    [Timestamp] public byte[] RowVersion { get; set; }   // SQL rowversion column
}
// UPDATE Orders SET ... WHERE Id=@id AND RowVersion=@original
// 0 rows affected → someone changed it → DbUpdateConcurrencyException
```

SQL Server auto-bumps `rowversion` on every write. EF Core turns the version into the WHERE; your job is the **catch**: reload + retry (last-write-wins-with-awareness), merge fields, or surface "reloaded — reapply your change" to the user. Web-friendly: no locks held while a human thinks (HTTP is stateless anyway — you *can't* hold a lock across requests sanely).

## Pessimistic concurrency (short critical sections only)

Prevent the conflict by locking up front — inside one transaction:

```sql
BEGIN TRAN;
SELECT @seat = Status FROM Seats WITH (UPDLOCK, HOLDLOCK) WHERE Id = @id;
IF @seat = 'Free' UPDATE Seats SET Status='Held', HeldBy=@user WHERE Id=@id;
COMMIT;   -- lock lifetime = milliseconds, not user think-time
```

`UPDLOCK` on the read prevents two sessions both seeing 'Free' and both updating (the read-then-write race — also the deadlock-prevention trick from that lesson). For queue-table consumers: `UPDLOCK, READPAST` = 'lock mine, skip others' locked rows' — the SQL-native work queue idiom.

## Choosing

| Scenario | Strategy |
|---|---|
| Human edit forms | Optimistic (rowversion) + good conflict UX |
| Inventory decrement, seat booking | Atomic UPDATE with guard (`SET Qty=Qty-1 WHERE Qty>0` — check @@ROWCOUNT) — often no explicit lock needed |
| Job/queue pickup | UPDLOCK + READPAST |
| Long business process 'locks' | Application-level (status column + owner + expiry), never DB locks |

The atomic-guard row deserves emphasis: a single conditional UPDATE is the cheapest correct concurrency control — the database's atomicity does all the work.
items.practiceTask: Add a rowversion concurrency token to one EF entity. Simulate the conflict: load in two contexts, save both, catch DbUpdateConcurrencyException and implement reload-and-inform handling. Then write the atomic inventory decrement and prove with two parallel tasks it never oversells.

quiz.question: UPDATE Stock SET Qty = Qty - 1 WHERE ProductId = @p AND Qty > 0 (checking rows affected) prevents overselling because:
options: The check and decrement are one atomic statement — no gap for another session to race into
quiz.explanation: Read-then-write in separate statements leaves a race window; one guarded UPDATE closes it with plain statement atomicity.
